narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including choosing full-time studies and building your undergraduate record. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 54% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your application—law schools use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that often predicts bar challenges. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the typical 3.21 average, actually worked in your favor by showing some academic resilience. Your LSAT score of 30, well under the dataset average of about 37, provided a modest counterbalance toward passing but wasn't strong enough to offset the concerns. Your low Year 1 decile ranking added a smaller push toward the failure prediction, while your full-time status and middle-income bracket had minimal sway, as they're common among applicants. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile stands out for lower decile placements early and late in school, unlike those in higher deciles who dominate the passing predictions. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and reapplying next cycle with those improvements to shift your predicted odds. You're capable of turning this around, and many students like you have succeeded by targeting these key areas. Keep pushing—law needs determined voices like yours.
